OMG, what's this new thing? This post takes a relaxed look at Google Apps Script — what it is. It's also really convenient in real-world dev. Let's see what Google Apps Script is.
Google Apps Script (GAS) is obviously a Google product. What can it do? Lots — for example, you can directly access Google Sheet content from Google Apps Script and do things with it.

What is Google Apps Script? In short, it's an online JavaScript IDE — you can write JavaScript and execute it directly online, integrated with Google services like Google Sheet, Google Drive, Google Calendar, etc.
So Google Apps Script suits making lightweight, simple little tools.
Let me list Google Apps Script's features:
Integration with Google services — e.g., directly used in Google Sheet
Suitable for lightweight, simple little tools
Can also use Google Apps Script's API for things like data fetching
Just need a Google account to use
The answer is obvious — also V8 engine. So you can use ES6 syntax in Google Apps Script —
So Google Apps Script is relatively easy for web devs to pick up — it's the JavaScript you're familiar with.
How to use Google Apps Script
How to start? Easy — just have a Google account and go to the
After entering, click "Start" at the top to enter the Google Apps Script management interface:
Click "New Project" to immediately open a new project. The screen should be self-explanatory since Google provides a Chinese interface — won't elaborate.
You can also use Google Apps Script + Google Sheet to build a simple API. If you're curious, see my post:
Build an API using Google Apps Script + Google Sheets (article in Mandarin)
That post is just a simple intro. In reality, you can do Post, Put, Delete — Google Apps Script is itself a backend environment, so you can do lots of things.
Google Apps Script is indeed a Serverless environment. But… what's Serverless?
In short, Serverless is an architecture where you don't spend time setting up your own server. Instead, you directly use cloud provider services like AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, etc.
Maybe a bit hard to understand why to use Serverless?

Here's an example. Say you have a small project that occasionally runs a scheduled task (Cron Job). Before Serverless, regardless of how small or fast your project is, you pay based on how long the server runs. Plus you have to specifically configure the server (referring to software server here). So you spend a lot of time managing the server instead of developing the project.

The advantage of Serverless is resources are only opened when needed and closed when not — so you only pay for resources you use. The expectation is you focus on developing the project rather than managing servers.

But Serverless has a few issues. Early dev models often required storing data on the server — user login info, user data, etc. But because Serverless is stateless, you have to use other approaches — like login switching to JWT (JSON Web Token). That's part of why JWT became so popular.

Serverless also pairs well with RESTful APIs since RESTful APIs are inherently stateless. Serverless is a great fit — only opens resources when you call the API instead of keeping resources open all the time. This significantly reduces resource waste and dev time spent managing servers.
OK with a basic understanding of Google Apps Script, let's move on to the next post.
